instruction: Write a short poem about a lighthouse.
output:
At the edge where land runs out of certainty,
the light keeps turning—
not asking if there are ships,
not wondering if they notice,
simply completing its slow circle
the way breathing completes itself,
the way tides do not pause
to see if the shore appreciates the effort.

Someone tends it.
Someone always has.

instruction: Write a haiku about an old library.
output:
Dust on the spines—
every untouched book still holds
its world inside, patient.

instruction: Write a haiku about the sea at dawn.
output:
Before the boats leave,
the harbor holds the whole sky—
then lets it go, quick.

instruction: Write a short poem about a garden tended across generations.
output:
She planted what her mother planted.
Her mother planted what her grandmother
brought from a village no longer on the map.

The persimmon tree does not know this.
It flowers anyway,
as it has always flowered,
before any of them came
and after all of them are gone.

instruction: Write a haiku about learning to ride a bicycle.
output:
Fall. Rise. Fall again.
Then—the moment the road
decides to hold you.

instruction: Write a poem about the last train on a closing line.
output:
The platform is the same.
The schedule is not.
Someone has chalked LAST RUN
on the board in careful letters
as if ceremony might soften it.

The conductor's voice is steady.
The passengers pretend to read.
Outside, the towns go by
the way they always did—
lit kitchen windows, dogs at fences,
the particular dark of fields at night.

At the end of the line,
no one is waiting.
The train sits in the quiet
for a minute longer than usual.

Then the lights go out.

instruction: Write a haiku about a clockmaker.
output:
His hands know the gears
better than his own heartbeat—
both keeping strange time.

instruction: Write a short poem about bread being baked.
output:
Before the city wakes,
the oven has been going three hours.

Flour and water and yeast—
the original chemistry,
older than writing,
older than money,

the slow conversation
between heat and grain
that ends the same way every time:
something simple
becoming something necessary.

instruction: Write a poem about two people saying goodbye at a train station.
output:
The announcement says five minutes.
We have been standing here
practicing for this.

I will say: take care.
You will say: I know.
We will mean something larger
than the words can hold.

The train will do what trains do.
The platform will empty.
I will stand here a moment longer
than necessary,
which is the only ceremony
I have left to offer.

instruction: Write a haiku about stars.
output:
The light you are seeing
left before you were born—
you are both still here.

instruction: Write a short poem about an empty chair.
output:
It holds the shape
of not being sat in.

The table is set for five.
The chair does not know this.
It simply stands
where it has always stood,
exact and patient,
waiting to be wrong about nothing.

instruction: Write a poem about someone learning a new skill late in life.
output:
She is sixty-three and learning the violin.
Her teacher is twenty-two
and has not yet learned
how not to wince.

It does not matter.
She practices in the kitchen
after dinner,
the bow finding its angle
the way a door finds its frame—
clumsily at first,
then with increasing certainty.

By spring she can play one song
from beginning to end
without stopping.

She plays it for no one
and for everyone.

instruction: Write a haiku about fog lifting over water.
output:
The fog lifts slowly.
What was hidden was always
there—waiting, patient.

instruction: Write a short poem about a letter that arrives too late.
output:
Thirty years in a drawer
in a house that has changed hands twice.

The handwriting is young—
you can tell by the pressure,
the way certainty leans into the pen.

The envelope is addressed
to someone who moved away
before it arrived,
who has since become
a different person entirely.

The letter says: I should have said this sooner.
The letter says: here is what I should have said.

It is read now, finally,
by someone who needed it
without knowing that they did.

instruction: Write a haiku about an old tree.
output:
The oak was here first.
It will still be here after.
We are the visitors.

instruction: Write a poem about the smell of a bakery in the morning.
output:
This is what three a.m. smells like
when someone has been working
while you were sleeping:

yeast and warmth and the particular sweetness
of something becoming itself,

the flour-dust air
that settles on everything
and is not unpleasant,

the window beginning to glow
from inside
before the street has decided
to be morning yet.

instruction: Write a haiku about the ocean at night.
output:
The dark water moves
whether or not anyone
stands here watching it.

instruction: Write a short poem from the perspective of an old lighthouse.
output:
I have outlasted the ships I saved
and the keepers who saved them.

The lens still turns.
The mechanism is simple:
a motor, a gear, a light—
and the dark, which is always there
and always the same size.

The ships are different now.
They navigate by satellite.
They do not need me.

I keep turning anyway.
This is what I know how to do.

instruction: Write a haiku about a detective examining a clue.
output:
One small thing out of place.
Everything else was arranged—
except this. Except this.

instruction: Write a poem about returning home after a long absence.
output:
The street is the same length.
The houses have been repainted.
The tree that was small is now
larger than the house beside it.

You are the only thing
that has changed in the way you expected.

The key still fits.
This is either comforting
or the saddest thing—
you cannot tell yet.

You put down your bag.
The house is quiet.
Outside, someone is mowing a lawn.

Somewhere in this
is the word for coming back
to a place that did not wait
but is nonetheless still here.
